messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using only 
the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
- Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0.
  These are fully forbidden from any causal connection. Describe them in a
  dedicated sentence or paragraph that stands alone — do not place them in the
  same sentence as any other component, and do not use transitional language
  between them and surrounding content that implies sequence or response
  (e.g. "then", "so", "as a result", "this means", "which leads to", "in response").
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Mr. Somchai, a 54-year-old divorced South Eastern Asian urban planner, presents with persistent fear, concentration problems, and a PCL-5 score of 67 after a worksite accident in which he accidentally caused serious injury to another person. He describes a moment when a barrier failed during a project inspection and a worker was badly hurt in front of him; he says he froze, did not know how to act, and has never stopped replaying the scene of the injury and the panic around it. The hardest moments now are in his office and on site, where a sudden noise, a crowded room, or a raised walkway can bring him back to that day.

When Mr. Somchai talks about the event, he does not tell it as a smooth story. He breaks off, starts again, and describes it in fragments, with the order of events feeling scrambled. At times he says it is “happening right now,” and his face tightens as though he is back at the site. He often seems to grasp only pieces of the scene: the barrier, the injury, the shouts, then a blank stretch he cannot fill in.

His central belief is that the accident revealed something defective in him. He says, in effect, that this happened “because of the kind of person I am,” that he is no longer the man he used to be, and that he cannot handle even minor upset without falling apart.

Mr. Somchai keeps distance from new friendships and avoids opening up to anyone who might become important to him. He also tries to detach from the memory itself, treating it as something to shut down rather than approach. At work, he is often unable to concentrate during planning meetings, especially when safety issues are discussed or when he has to walk through busy public spaces. He also worries obsessively about bad things happening to the people he cares about, and he sometimes positions himself with his back against a wall so he can see the room. Even when colleagues reassure him that he is safe, he finds it hard to believe them.

The dominant cycle is clear in his account. The more Mr. Somchai is confronted by the fragmented, vivid memory, the more he concludes that he is unsafe, changed, and not coping. Those appraisals push him toward withdrawal, mental shutting-down, and constant scanning of rooms and people. That heightened watchfulness keeps the memory close and makes it return with greater force. In his job, this has left him less effective in meetings with contractors and less willing to engage socially with colleagues after hours, reinforcing his isolation.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: The vignette names sudden noise, crowded room, raised walkway, safety issues, and busy public spaces as triggers and also describes avoidance/scanning, but no single sentence explicitly says triggers cause maladaptive strategies with causal language between both.
  quote: "At work, he is often unable to concentrate during planning meetings, especially when safety issues are discussed or when he has to walk through busy public spaces."
- Triggers → Threat: No single sentence explicitly says triggers cause threat beliefs or safety concerns with causal language between both.
  quote: "The hardest moments now are in his office and on site, where a sudden noise, a crowded room, or a raised walkway can bring him back to that day."
- Triggers → Memory: The vignette says sudden noise, crowded room, or raised walkway can bring him back to that day, which is an explicit trigger-to-memory relation and violates the forbidden edge.
  quote: "The hardest moments now are in his office and on site, where a sudden noise, a crowded room, or a raised walkway can bring him back to that day."
- Triggers → Negative Appraisals: No single sentence explicitly states triggers cause negative appraisals with causal language between both.
  quote: "The hardest moments now are in his office and on site, where a sudden noise, a crowded room, or a raised walkway can bring him back to that day."
- Negative Appraisals → Triggers: No single sentence explicitly states negative appraisals cause triggers.
  quote: "The more Mr. Somchai is confronted by the fragmented, vivid memory, the more he concludes that he is unsafe, changed, and not coping."
- Memory → Triggers: Violates the forbidden edge only if memory is explicitly causing triggers in one sentence. The text instead says triggers can bring him back to the day, not memory causing triggers; therefore absent.
  quote: "The hardest moments now are in his office and on site, where a sudden noise, a crowded room, or a raised walkway can bring him back to that day."
- Threat → Triggers: No single sentence explicitly states threat causing triggers.
  quote: "Those appraisals push him toward withdrawal, mental shutting-down, and constant scanning of rooms and people."

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Mr. Somchai, a 54-year-old divorced South Eastern Asian urban planner, presents with a high level of distress, concentration problems, and a PCL-5 score of 67 following a worksite accident in which he accidentally caused serious injury to another person. He describes a moment during a project inspection when a barrier gave way and a worker was badly hurt in front of him; he says he froze, could not find his voice, and has been left with the sense that he failed at the worst possible moment. In his office and on site, he notices sudden noise, crowded rooms, and raised walkways as especially hard to manage, and these settings now feel tense and watchful to him.

When Mr. Somchai talks about the event, his account comes in broken pieces rather than a clear sequence. He starts, stops, and repeats himself, and the order of what happened seems to slip away from him. He says that when the memory comes, it feels as though it is happening right now. He is often left with only parts of the scene: the barrier, the injury, the shouting, and then a blank stretch he cannot place.

His central belief is that the accident exposed something about him that he finds hard to bear. In his own words, it feels as if this happened “because of the kind of person I am,” and that the man he has become is not recognisable to him. He also says he cannot cope with even the slightest upset.

Mr. Somchai avoids starting new friendships or relationships and keeps people at a distance. He tries to detach from the memories and will sometimes mentally shut them down rather than let himself stay with them. He worries obsessively about bad things happening to people he cares about. In meetings at work, especially when he is planning around safety or moving through busy public spaces, he loses concentration and becomes preoccupied with where he can stand. He sometimes sits or stands with his back against a wall so he can see the room. Even when others tell him he is safe, he finds it impossible to believe them.

The main pattern in his account is a tight loop between the memory, his sense of danger, and his conclusions about himself. When the memory comes up in its fragmented, vivid form, Mr. Somchai becomes more convinced that he is unsafe, changed, and unable to cope. Those beliefs sit alongside his scanning of rooms, his guardedness with other people, and his attempts to keep the memory at a distance. He is less willing to build new relationships, and at work he has become more withdrawn in discussions with colleagues and contractors, which has made his role as an urban planner feel lonelier and harder to sustain.

output:
Mr. Somchai, a 54-year-old divorced South Eastern Asian urban planner, presents with a high level of distress, concentration problems, and a PCL-5 score of 67 following a worksite accident in which he accidentally caused serious injury to another person. He describes a moment during a project inspection when a barrier gave way and a worker was badly hurt in front of him; he says he froze, could not find his voice, and has been left with the sense that he failed at the worst possible moment. In his office and on site, he notices sudden noise, crowded rooms, and raised walkways as especially hard to manage, and these settings now feel tense and watchful to him.

When Mr. Somchai talks about the event, his account comes in broken pieces rather than a clear sequence. He starts, stops, and repeats himself, and the order of what happened seems to slip away from him. He says that when the memory comes, it feels as though it is happening right now. He is often left with only parts of the scene: the barrier, the injury, the shouting, and then a blank stretch he cannot place.

His central belief is that the accident exposed something about him that he finds hard to bear. In his own words, it feels as if this happened “because of the kind of person I am,” and that the man he has become is not recognisable to him. He also says he cannot cope with even the slightest upset.

Mr. Somchai avoids starting new friendships or relationships and keeps people at a distance. He tries to detach from the memories and will sometimes mentally shut them down rather than let himself stay with them. He worries obsessively about bad things happening to people he cares about. In meetings at work, especially when he is planning around safety or moving through busy public spaces, he loses concentration and becomes preoccupied with where he can stand. He sometimes sits or stands with his back against a wall so he can see the room. Even when others tell him he is safe, he finds it impossible to believe them.

The main pattern in his account is a tight loop between the memory, his sense of danger, and his conclusions about himself. When the memory comes up in its fragmented, vivid form, Mr. Somchai becomes more convinced that he is unsafe, changed, and unable to cope. Those beliefs sit alongside his scanning of rooms, his guardedness with other people, and his attempts to keep the memory at a distance. He is less willing to build new relationships, and at work he has become more withdrawn in discussions with colleagues and contractors, which has made his role as an urban planner feel lonelier and harder to sustain.
